beads: file ft-4f1fv (workflows handlers test CompatRuntime import)

File ft-4f1fv open/P2/bug: "workflows handlers test build misses
CompatRuntime import for Runtime::block_on". This is the bead for
the fix that landed in 760b2e5b earlier in this session — filing the
bead after the fix so the tracker has a record of the exact failure
class for future reference and so a parallel agent that hits the
same symptom in another test module has a grep target to confirm
the fix has been applied consistently.

Left open pending a sweep across the rest of the workflows/ test
files to confirm no other helpers are missing the same import.

Pure metadata. No source files.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-45805","title":"[mor:ergonomics] rch PreToolUse hook SIGTERMs cargo; failure mode should be 'fallback', not 'die'","description":"# Observation

From working memory + user experience across recent sessions:

- rch PreToolUse hook (\`force_remote=true\` in
  ~/.config/rch/config.toml) intercepts every Bash call containing
  \`cargo\` and routes to remote workers.
- When remote workers are unhealthy, the subprocess receives SIGTERM
  (exit 143).
- Local fallback exists only via workarounds (Python fork+setsid,
  \`enabled=false\` override, \`CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-<name>\`
  bypass).

At 3am: operator needs to reproduce a test failure. Runs
\`cargo test foo\`. Shell returns exit 143 with no diagnostic.
Operator has to know the workaround exists. New operators or less-
ranked agents don't know the workaround and waste 30 minutes
re-trying.

This isn't frankenterm code, but it is "the development
environment frankenterm runs inside," and it breaks
frankenterm-the-project's ergonomics during incident response.

# Question

Two levers worth exploring:

1. **Fallback-on-worker-failure in rch itself.** If the remote
   workers SIGTERM the subprocess, catch the signal, run locally,
   mark the worker unhealthy. Exit 143 becomes a last resort,
   not the default failure mode.

2. **Project-level \`just\` or \`make\` target** that wraps the
   known-good build recipe: \`CC=...clang CXX=...clang++
   CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-local-target cargo <args>\`. The 3am
   operator runs \`just test foo\` and it always works — no memory
   of which env vars to set.

Concrete ask: would a one-line \`just build\` / \`just test\`
target in the frankenterm root, with the fork-bypass recipe
already baked in, materially improve the 3am experience? It's
~15 min of work and saves 30 min per incident per operator.

# 3am principle

Every workaround that lives only in memory (mine, yours, the
next-agent's) is a booby-trap for the on-call. Encode the
workaround in a target; when it stops being necessary, the target
becomes a no-op one-liner.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:41.686932Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:49:54.612291Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:49:54.611695Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["3am","ergonomics","mor","rch","tooling"]}

 {"id":"ft-4qom2","title":"[SEV] tmux cmd_queue cap only enforced at pop site; unbounded growth when state != Idle","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs TmuxDomainState.cmd_queue has CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000 enforced only inside send_next_command at line 289 via cmd_queue.drain(..excess). send_next_command early-returns at line 286-288 when state != Idle, so the drain-oldest logic never runs if tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse.

Four push sites append to cmd_queue with NO cap check:
- line 196: Event::LayoutChange -> ListAllPanes
- line 223: Event::SessionChanged -> ListCommands
- line 234: Event::WindowAdd -> ListAllWindows
- line 375: split_pane (user split) -> SplitPane

Concrete interleaving: (1) tmux sends a command, enters State::WaitingForResponse; (2) tmux server is slow/hung (real-world scenario: remote tmux on lagging SSH link, tmux server overloaded, Event::Guarded response delayed); (3) GUI user resizes window repeatedly -> stream of Event::LayoutChange -> each push_back at line 196 runs without cap check; (4) send_next_command early-returns because state != Idle. Queue grows unbounded -> memory leak, eventual OOM.

The CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH comment at line 32-33 explicitly advertises 'prevent unbounded memory growth during event storms' as a safety invariant. That invariant is currently only upheld at the pop site, where it can't run under the exact condition (stuck tmux) that event storms exploit.

Fix: extract the cap-enforcement logic into a push_command_capped helper that applies the drain-oldest + warn/error logging at every push site. The helper runs under the cmd_queue lock (just acquired by the caller), so it's atomic with the push and has no additional locking cost.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:17:26.601099Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.160715Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.158111Z","close_reason":"Completed: added push_command_capped() helper and routed all 4 production push sites (LayoutChange/SessionChanged/WindowAdd/split_pane) through it. The hard cap is now enforced at push time, not only at pop time — closing the unbounded-growth window when tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse. cargo check passes clean. Commit 1055cffc.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-d0ez0.2","title":"20-pane integration test: multi-agent detection, workflow trigger, policy gate, FTS search verification","description":"# Goal
Scale the E2E proof to 20 panes with mixed agent types, verifying all major subsystems integrate correctly under realistic multi-agent conditions.

# Background
The minimal E2E (ft-d0ez0.1) proves the basic loop. This test proves it works at moderate scale with mixed agent types, workflow triggers, policy enforcement, and FTS search.

# Design
## Test script: tests/e2e/e2e_20pane_integration.py

### Setup
- 20 panes via headless mux server: 7 simulated Codex, 7 Claude Code, 3 Gemini, 3 plain shell
- Each agent pane receives fixture output from crates/frankenterm-core/tests/corpus/<agent>/
- Output mix: rate limits, errors, normal output, long-running ops

### Policy configuration (ft.toml for the test)
Pane 15 is policy-denied via safety config:
  [safety]
  require_prompt_active = false
  block_alt_screen = true
  [safety.rate_limits]
  send_text = { max_per_second = 0 }  # deny all sends to trigger rate limit
  # OR use a custom policy rule that denies pane_id=15 specifically

### Verification matrix (8 checks)
1. Delta extraction: ft robot get-text --all --tail 1 returns data for all 20 panes
2. Pattern detection: ft robot events --limit 50 returns rate-limit events for 17 agent panes
3. False positive check: ft robot events filtered to panes 17,18,19 returns ZERO events
4. Workflow trigger: ft workflow status shows at least 1 completed workflow execution
5. Policy gate: ft robot send 15 'test' returns ok:false with error.code=robot.policy_denied
6. FTS search: ft robot search 'rate limit' returns results spanning multiple panes
7. Robot Mode state: ft robot state returns all 20 panes with correct metadata (domain, title)
8. Audit trail: ft audit --decision deny shows the pane 15 denial record

### Timing
- Stagger pane output over 10 seconds to test temporal ordering
- Total duration target: under 60 seconds

## Logging
- JSON report: tests/e2e/output/e2e_20pane_<timestamp>.json
- Per-check: check_name, expected, actual, pass/fail, duration_ms, raw_response (first 500 chars)
- Summary: total_checks, passed, failed, elapsed_total_ms
- On failure: dump ft doctor --json, full event list, audit trail, failing check raw response

# Acceptance Criteria
1. All 8 verification checks pass.
2. Zero false-positive detections on plain shell panes.
3. At least 1 completed workflow in execution log.
4. Audit trail includes policy denial with pane_id=15.
5. Structured JSON report produced.
6. Completes in under 60 seconds.
7. On failure, diagnostic output pinpoints exactly which check failed.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-04-06T18:57:05.594187Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-11T01:32:02.956948Z","closed_at":"2026-04-11T01:32:02.956583Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","integration","multi-agent"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-d0ez0.2","depends_on_id":"ft-d0ez0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:57:05.594187Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-d0ez0.2","depends_on_id":"ft-d0ez0.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:57:25.496062Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-d0ez0.3","title":"50-pane stress test: fleet memory pressure transitions, tiered scrollback eviction, backpressure under load","description":"# Goal
Prove fleet memory management and tiered scrollback work under sustained 50-pane load, matching the advertised 200+ pane scale claims.

# Background
The README claims '200+ panes under 1GB' and describes a 4-tier fleet memory controller. The fleet_memory_controller.rs and scrollback_tiers.rs are implemented, but never tested at scale. This test proves they work.

# Design
## Test script: tests/e2e/e2e_50pane_stress.py
### Setup
- 50 panes via headless mux server, each generating ~1KB/sec continuous output via script
- Per-pane memory budget configured to trigger tier transitions within 5 minutes
- Total sustained throughput: ~50KB/sec

### Phase 1: Warm-up (0-60s)
- All panes generating output
- Verify: ft robot state shows 50 panes, ft doctor --json shows Normal pressure tier

### Phase 2: Pressure build (60-180s)
- Continue output generation
- Monitor: FleetPressureTier transitions via ft doctor --json (polled every 10s)
- Expected: Normal -> Elevated as scrollback accumulates

### Phase 3: Tier transitions (180-300s)
- Continue output generation with increased rate on 10 panes
- Expected: Elevated -> Critical on high-output panes
- Verify: tiered scrollback eviction events occur (warm tier activation)

### Phase 4: Detection under load (at 240s)
- Inject rate-limit pattern into pane 25
- Measure: time from injection to ft robot events showing the detection
- Expected: detection latency < 5 seconds

### Phase 5: Robot Mode under load (at 270s)
- Time ft robot state, ft robot search, ft robot get-text responses
- Expected: state < 2s, search < 5s

### Metrics collection
- Peak RSS of ft process (via ps or /proc/self/status)
- Detection latency (injection time vs event timestamp)
- Robot Mode response latency
- Pressure tier transition timestamps
- Scrollback tier eviction counts

## Logging
- JSON metrics report: tests/e2e/output/e2e_50pane_stress_<timestamp>.json
- Per-phase: phase_name, start_time, end_time, metrics_snapshot
- Timeseries: pressure_tier sampled every 10s
- On failure: full metrics dump + ft doctor --json + process info

# Acceptance Criteria
1. ft process RSS stays under 1GB with 50 panes over 5 minutes.
2. Pattern detection latency < 5 seconds under 50-pane load.
3. Robot Mode state query < 2 seconds, search < 5 seconds under load.
4. At least one FleetPressureTier transition above Normal observed.
5. At least one tiered scrollback tier transition (hot->warm) observed.
6. No crashes, panics, or data corruption during 5-minute run.
7. Machine-readable metrics report produced with all measurements.
8. On failure, report identifies which metric breached which threshold.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":360,"created_at":"2026-04-06T18:57:21.555623Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-11T02:06:29.576005Z","closed_at":"2026-04-11T02:06:29.575707Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","fleet-memory","performance","scrollback","stress"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-d0ez0.3","depends_on_id":"ft-d0ez0","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:57:21.555623Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"ft-d0ez0.3","depends_on_id":"ft-d0ez0.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:57:25.736976Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2919,"issue_id":"ft-d0ez0.3","author":"jemanuel","text":"CI note: This 5-minute test should run in a nightly CI job (not per-push) or be triggered manually.  {"id":"ft-d0ez0.4","title":"E2E proof for ft tx run: execute a real transactional multi-pane operation and verify audit trail","description":"# Goal
Prove that ft tx run executes real pane operations transactionally, including compensation on failure, with a complete audit trail.

# Background
The tx integration tests (ft-y9lnb.5) use MockWezterm. This E2E test uses real (or headless mux server) panes to prove the full CLI->executor->pane->audit pipeline works.

# Verified Contract Format (MissionTxContract JSON)
{
  "tx_version": 1,
  "intent": { "tx_id": "test-tx-1", "requested_by": "operator", "summary": "E2E test", "correlation_id": "e2e-001", "created_at_ms": <timestamp> },
  "plan": {
    "plan_id": "plan-1", "tx_id": "test-tx-1",
    "steps": [
      { "step_id": "step-1", "ordinal": 0, "action": { "type": "send_text", "pane_id": 0, "text": "echo step1-marker" }, "description": "Send marker to pane 0" },
      { "step_id": "step-2", "ordinal": 1, "action": { "type": "send_text", "pane_id": 1, "text": "echo step2-marker" }, "description": "Send marker to pane 1" }
    ],
    "preconditions": [],
    "compensations": [
      { "for_step_id": "step-1", "action": { "type": "send_text", "pane_id": 0, "text": "echo COMPENSATED" } }
    ]
  },
  "lifecycle_state": "pending",
  "outcome": "pending",
  "receipts": []
}

# Design
## Test script: tests/e2e/e2e_tx_run.py

### Scenario 1: Happy path (2-step transaction)
1. Start ft watch with headless mux server
2. Create 2 panes with shell sessions
3. Write mission.json contract (template above)
4. Run ft tx run --contract mission.json
5. Verify: ft robot get-text 0 contains 'step1-marker'
6. Verify: ft robot get-text 1 contains 'step2-marker'
7. Verify: ft tx show --include-contract shows committed state

### Scenario 2: Partial failure with compensation
1. Same setup, but step 2 targets nonexistent pane 99
2. Run ft tx run --contract mission_fail.json
3. Verify: pane 0 contains 'COMPENSATED' (compensation ran)
4. Verify: ft tx show shows compensation state
5. Verify: ft audit shows failure + compensation records

### Scenario 3: Policy denial
1. Configure ft.toml safety to deny SendText on pane 1
2. Run ft tx run --contract mission.json
3. Verify: prepare-phase denial with policy reason in output
4. Verify: pane 0 does NOT contain 'step1-marker' (no steps executed on denial)

## Logging
- JSON report: tests/e2e/output/e2e_tx_run_<timestamp>.json
- Per-scenario: scenario_name, contract_file, ft_tx_run_stdout, ft_tx_run_stderr, ft_tx_show_output, audit_records, pane_contents, pass/fail
- On failure: full diagnostic dump

# Acceptance Criteria
1. All 3 scenarios pass.
2. Happy path shows markers in panes AND committed state in ft tx show.
3. Compensation scenario shows compensation execution in audit trail AND pane output.
4. Policy denial shows zero pane modifications.
5. Completes in under 60 seconds.
6.  {"id":"ft-dqsev","title":"[bug] delete_session runs 3 DELETEs without a transaction — partial failure yields orphaned checkpoints","description":"# Bug

\`session_restore::delete_session\` at \`crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:1003\`:

\`\`\`rust
pub fn delete_session(db_path: &str, session_id: &str) -> Result<bool, RestoreError> {
    let conn = open_conn(db_path)?;
    conn.execute("DELETE FROM mux_pane_state WHERE checkpoint_id IN ...", [session_id])?;
    conn.execute("DELETE FROM session_checkpoints WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    let deleted = conn.execute("DELETE FROM mux_sessions WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    Ok(deleted > 0)
}
\`\`\`

Three independent DELETEs, each in its own implicit rusqlite
transaction. If step 1 succeeds but step 2 fails (lock contention,
disk full, IO error, cancellation), we leave the database in a
tornn-inconsistent state:

  - Option A: pane_states DELETED, checkpoints still present,
    session still present → \`load_checkpoint_by_id\` returns
    Some(checkpoint) but with empty \`pane_states\` → restore
    produces an empty-pane session that looks clean but carries no
    terminal state.

  - Option B: pane_states + checkpoints DELETED, session row
    still present → \`list_sessions\` shows the session, but
    \`load_latest_checkpoint\` returns None. Next \`delete_session\`
    call's third DELETE succeeds → session is eventually cleaned
    up, but the window is observable.

The other deletion-style paths in session_restore.rs DO wrap their
mutations in \`conn.transaction()\` (see lines 626, 673). delete_session
is the anomaly.

# Fix

Wrap the three DELETEs in a single \`conn.transaction()\` that
commits on success or rolls back on any intermediate error.

\`\`\`rust
pub fn delete_session(db_path: &str, session_id: &str) -> Result<bool, RestoreError> {
    let mut conn = open_conn(db_path)?;
    let tx = conn.transaction()?;
    tx.execute("DELETE FROM mux_pane_state WHERE checkpoint_id IN ...", [session_id])?;
    tx.execute("DELETE FROM session_checkpoints WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    let deleted = tx.execute("DELETE FROM mux_sessions WHERE session_id = ?1", [session_id])?;
    tx.commit()?;
    Ok(deleted > 0)
}
\`\`\`

# Test

Regression test: inject mid-operation failure (e.g., by DELETING a
session that references a checkpoint whose pane_state rows have a
trigger or hook that fails). Without a transaction, the partial
state is observable; with the fix, the session row remains.

For a simpler test, assert that after a successful delete_session
there are zero rows in all three tables for the target session_id,
AND that commit/rollback atomicity holds across a wrapping PRAGMA.

Single function, ~6 LOC change. No schema migration. Preserves the

 {"id":"ft-mdaon","title":"[MEDIUM] escape-parser robustness suite contains vacuous CSI MR and missing concatenation property","description":"[MEDIUM] Recent commit f3242133 (test: proptest robustness suite for escape-parser) overstates the protection added by frankenterm/escape-parser/tests/proptest_parser_robustness.rs.

Evidence:
- The file header claims property 3: `Concatenation consistency — parse(a++b) == parse(a) ++ parse(b)` (lines 9-10), but there is no corresponding test anywhere in the file. The suite advertises a metamorphic relation it never checks.
- The new `csi_roundtrip_via_display` property is partially vacuous (lines 170-184). It only asserts equality when `actions1.len() == 1` AND `actions2.len() == 1`; otherwise it silently skips the case. A regression that starts emitting zero or multiple actions for generated CSI sequences would therefore pass this property instead of failing.
- The generated inputs in `arb_csi_sequence()` are always syntactically complete CSI byte strings (lines 30-47), so a non-singleton parse result is exactly the kind of behavioral drift this MR should catch, not skip.

Why this matters:
- This commit is framed as a robustness/metamorphic suite, but two of the strongest claimed relations are currently absent or weak enough to miss structural parser regressions.
- Escape parsing is a streaming state machine; vacuous MRs give false confidence around output-shape stability.

Requested fix:
- Add the missing concatenation-consistency property or remove the claim from the suite header/commit description.
- Tighten `csi_roundtrip_via_display` so generated complete CSI inputs must roundtrip to exactly one action on both parses, and fail otherwise.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T05:56:59.004870Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T18:11:19.890939Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T18:11:19.890629Z","close_reason":"Already fixed by db835e7b — csi_roundtrip_via_display tightened with arb_singleton_csi_sequence + hard asserts, concatenation_consistency_for_printable added","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

Fix the known test compilation/runtime errors in mcp.rs that prevent the 170 test functions from running with default features enabled.

# Background
MEMORY.md documents: mcp.rs has pre-existing test errors with default features. The MCP module (3,229 lines, 170 #[test] functions) has comprehensive test coverage. MCP submodules (mcp_bridge, mcp_framework, mcp_helpers, etc.) are declared inside mcp.rs via #[path], NOT in lib.rs — this means feature-gate interactions may affect module resolution.

# Design
## Diagnostic steps
1. Run: cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib mcp 2>&1 | tee /tmp/mcp_errors.log

2. Categorize errors:
   - Type mismatches from recent module changes -> update test expectations
   - Missing types/imports due to feature gates -> add #[cfg(feature)] or fix import paths
   - Async runtime errors (#[tokio::test] vs asupersync) -> migrate to sync or runtime_compat
   - Stale test data referencing old API shapes -> update fixtures
3. Fix each error, verifying after each fix that the change compiles
4. Final run: cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib mcp -- all 170 tests green

## Common failure patterns to watch for
- MCP submodules use #[path] declarations — if a submodule file moves or renames, the path attribute breaks
- The 57 tokio-based test failures (ft-22x4r) may overlap — if MCP tests use #[tokio::test], coordinate with ft-682n6
- Feature flag interactions: some test helper types may only exist under specific features (mcp, subprocess-bridge, etc.)
- robot_types.rs changes may have updated MCP response envelope shapes without updating mcp.rs test expectations

## Structured Logging
- Diagnostic log: mcp_test_fix_<timestamp>.log with each error, category, fix applied
- Before/after test counts to verify no tests lost

# Acceptance Criteria
1. cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib mcp passes with 0 failures under default features.
2. All 170 test functions execute (none skipped, none #[ignore]d without documented reason).
3. No semantic changes to test intent — only update expectations to match current API.
4. Diagnostic log documenting each fix for future reference.
5.  {"id":"ft-n3mim","title":"[mor:ergonomics] Error::Runtime(String) is the '3am diagnostic dead-end' variant","description":"# Observation

Memory and grep confirm the frankenterm-core Error enum has no
\`Generic\` variant — the convention is \`Runtime(String)\` for
ad-hoc failures. Every time a caller needed a fresh error shape and
didn't want to define a typed variant, they reached for
\`Error::Runtime\`.

At 3am during an incident, the operator sees:

    Error: runtime: mux pool write failed: io error: Broken pipe (os error 32)

This is a String wrapped in an enum variant. You can't match on it,
you can't branch on it programmatically, you can't build a
dashboard row off it. The information that would make the incident
tractable — is this a transient network blip, a crashed mux, a
kernel panic, a rate limit, a disk-full? — is buried in a
string the operator has to grep for.

# Question

For each subsystem that currently stuffs into \`Error::Runtime(...)\`,
is there a typed variant that would make triage faster? Specific
candidates worth auditing:

- \`runtime_compat\` cancel / timeout wrappers — currently stringify
  the underlying failure into Runtime
- \`mux_pool\` write/read failures that aren't already covered by
  \`WeztermError\`
- \`asupersync\` lifecycle errors the caller needs to distinguish
  from transient I/O

A principle: every error the operator would act on differently
deserves a typed variant. Everything else can stay generic.

# 3am impact

This is the class of issue that doesn't show up in normal workflow.
It only bites when production is on fire. The cost is invisible
until the cost is enormous. The fix is incremental: every Runtime
creation site is a candidate for typing, and typing them one-at-a-

 {"id":"ft-qf3vk","title":"[HIGH] ft-ybtyg incomplete: session_checkpoints.state_hash is written but never verified on load","description":"Follow-up from ft-ybtyg (closed in 598a7c13).

## Gap

The ft-ybtyg fix replaced the placeholder `'restore'` literal with a real SipHash via `compute_restore_state_hash` and documented the intent: 'a later integrity check can recompute and compare'. But grep across session_restore.rs + snapshot_engine.rs + storage.rs finds ZERO callers that read the stored state_hash, recompute from the current inputs, and assert equality. The hash is written on INSERT, never verified on SELECT/load.

Result: the on-disk state_hash column now contains useful-looking SipHash values but is still effectively unverified — an attacker who tampers with the session_checkpoints row (flipping hash + any other field) is not detected, because no read path compares the stored hash against the recomputed hash.

## Reproducer
```
grep -rn 'compute_restore_state_hash\|compute_state_hash' crates/frankenterm-core/src/ | grep -iE 'verify|check|compare|assert_eq'
```
Expected matches: any call site that does `recomputed == stored`.
Actual: (empty).

## Fix direction
1. In the session-load path (session_restore.rs load fn + snapshot_engine.rs open/restore), after reading a session_checkpoints row, call compute_restore_state_hash on the current inputs and compare with the stored state_hash column.
2. On mismatch, return a typed Error (new StateHashMismatch variant) and refuse to restore from a tampered checkpoint.
3. Add regression test with a deliberately-tampered row that must produce StateHashMismatch and NOT a silent restore.

## Severity
MEDIUM → HIGH. The fix closed the docs-drift half but left the verification half open. A partial integrity check is worse than no check because it signals a stronger trust story than actually exists.

## Priority
P1 — this closes a real hole that ft-ybtyg was supposed to close.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:03:26.056799Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:28:42.029163Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:28:42.028931Z","close_reason":"fixed in ca3d3fe2 — state_hash verification wired into load_checkpoint_by_id for startup rows; StateHashMismatch error + 5 regression tests (tampered hash, tampered pane_id_map, legacy 'restore' literal, null metadata, non-startup skip). Periodic/capture checkpoints remain a documented follow-up since their hash is over PaneInfo not reconstructable from session_checkpoints alone.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-qgk35","title":"[mor:security-engineering] wa.send trust boundary is 'which agent', not 'what bytes'","description":"# Observation

`WaSendTool` (mcp_tools.rs:1819) caps payload at 4 MiB (ft-05hfm) and
gates via the policy engine. Everything the authenticated agent
submits as \`text\` is then forwarded through
\`wezterm cli send-text -- <text>\` to the target pane.

The Command-args layering is safe from shell injection: args are
passed directly, not through \`sh -c\`. But send-text is typing
bytes into a terminal, and those bytes include the full control-char
space:

- \\x03 (Ctrl+C) kills the foreground process
- \\x04 (Ctrl+D) sends EOF, often exiting shells/REPLs
- \\x1a (Ctrl+Z) suspends
- Escape sequences can fake prompt text (`\\x1b[H\\x1b[2J$ `)
- Bracketed-paste wrappers can be forged

Trust model today: "if the agent is authenticated and policy allows
scope, arbitrary bytes are fine." This is appropriate for the
normal case (an agent typing \`npm test\` into its own pane). It is
less obviously appropriate for:

- agent A sending into agent B's pane (policy gates scope but not
  content)
- a compromised/misconfigured agent signing off its scope with a
  ghost Ctrl+C
- typing \`\\x1bc\` (reset terminal) into a neighbor pane to mask
  the audit trail of whatever just scrolled off

# Question

Would a lightweight content class — "does this payload contain
control characters outside the normal typing range?" — as a
separate policy capability be worth the complexity? The default
policy could allow control chars (current behavior); a hardening
toggle could require an explicit \`send-control-chars\` capability.

Agents that need to Ctrl+C their own long-running commands would
declare the capability up front; the 99% of typing-plain-text cases
would run without it and be structurally incapable of sending
Ctrl+C into another pane.

# Trust boundary

Current boundary: authenticated identity + policy scope + size cap.
Open question: should the boundary also include "which byte classes
can flow"? For security platforms, the answer is usually yes; the
tradeoff is ergonomics cost during normal agent operation.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:28.713563Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:57.980813Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:30.134277Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mcp","mor","policy","security"],"comments":[{"id":3601,"issue_id":"ft-qgk35","author":"content-trust-audit","text":"answered — investigation found content-aware gating already exists via PolicyEngine::analyze_content_risk (policy.rs:4792) + PredicateKind::CommandPattern (policy.rs:3296). Trust boundary IS identity + bytes, not identity alone. Specific extension to cover terminal-control bytes (Ctrl+C/D/Z, ESC, bracketed-paste wraps) filed as child ft-qgk35.1 (P2) with ~10-line patch + 5 tests.","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:02:57Z"}]}

 {"id":"ft-wajba","title":"tmux cmd_queue drop-head-in-flight corrupts Guarded response routing","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs:73-91 (push_command_capped) vs :165-186 (Guarded response handler).

push_command_capped(queue, cmd) does queue.drain(..excess) when queue exceeds CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000. The drain is from the FRONT. But when TmuxDomainState.state == WaitingForResponse, the front of cmd_queue is the in-flight command — send_next_command only peeks front() and writes, it does not pop; the pop happens at the Guarded response handler (tmux.rs:172) via cmd_queue.pop_front().

Race / correctness gap: if push_command_capped runs while state == WaitingForResponse and queue.len() reaches 50_001, drain drops the in-flight head. When the Guarded response arrives, pop_front() pops a DIFFERENT pending command and calls cmd.process_result(domain_id, &response) on the wrong command — cross-wiring response payload onto the wrong command parser. Both threads hold the cmd_queue lock so the drain+pop ordering is deterministic under the lock, but the correctness violation stands.

Fix options (not applied here, pattern-level):
 1. When state == WaitingForResponse, skip index 0 in drain: queue.drain(1..=excess) (but preserves head).
 2. Drain from the back (newest-first) which discards most recently enqueued layout/session events that often re-fire.
 3. Abort the in-flight state machine (set state=Idle) and drain — heavy-handed but restores invariant.

Only triggers in pathological event storms (remote tmux lag + 50_000 queued commands) so severity is MEDIUM. The original bug fixed in 1055cffc (push-site cap enforcement) traded unbounded memory for this subtle correctness edge. A regression test would drive tmux into WaitingForResponse, push >50_001 Layout/SessionChanged events, then fire a Guarded response and assert process_result is NOT called against a different command type than the one that was sent.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:16:32.972876Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:41.760204Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:41.759836Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 9cc7e6c2. push_command_capped migrated from free function to TmuxDomainState method so it can consult self.state before choosing drain range:
- state == WaitingForResponse → drain(1..=excess) (preserves index 0, the in-flight cmd awaiting its Guarded response)
- any other state → drain(..excess) (retains existing drop-oldest semantics)

Both drain branches log distinctly so event-storm incidents are traceable. Lock ordering matches Guarded-handler's (cmd_queue first, state transiently inside method): no deadlock. send_next_command's cap-drain is untouched — already guarded by early 'state != Idle → return'.

Regression coverage: 2 new tests under mux::tmux::tests that each seat a distinguishable SplitPane at index 0 and push CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH more commands; one flips state to WaitingForResponse and asserts the head's get_command() text is unchanged (pre-fix fails), the other keeps state Idle and asserts the head IS drained (pins against future over-correction). Existing cmd_queue_hard_cap_drains_oldest_when_exceeded still green after the refactor.

Verification via rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-p5-orch-target:
- cargo check -p mux --lib: clean
- cargo test -p mux --lib cmd_queue: 3/3 green.

Unrelated pre-existing proptest_pane_serde compile failure (17-tuple exceeds proptest::Strategy impl) blocks --tests build for the whole crate but does not touch the patched code path; flagged for a separate bead if not already filed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
